data.title: Sempra: Key Player In A Vital Industry That's Potentially Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Undergoing a significant investment cycle that comes with a lot of potential, Sempra (NYSE:SRE) is definitely an interesting company to look into, especially since they’re the largest energy transmission and distribution company in Texas and has significant stakes in California, as well as several LNG/natural gas pipelines and storage facilities, on top of renewables. With nice potential to grow in a vital market, a decent dividend and quite an attractive valuation, I rate SRE stock a Buy now, with good potential to see it recover if their plan works well.

We see little growth in their revenue throughout the past decade, but their operating income managed to improve significantly. Their net income shows clear improvements, although that’s not really that good for a whole decade. However, things are about to change, as the company is currently in a very significant investment cycle which they expect to be very beneficial to their EPS, as I’ll describe below.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see that they aren’t in the best position when looking at the current assets that are below the current liabilities, but a big portion of the latter is in current portion of long-term debt, which they just refinance (although at worse rates, and the interest is now at over $1.1 billion). Still, there’s a very significant amount of assets that cover the total liabilities, leaving us with $38.2 billion in equity, traded at a market cap of ~$49.7 billion, so a PB ratio of 1.3, which isn’t the most expensive. So, not the worst balance sheet for a utility company, and I believe it’s getting to quite an attractive level given the PB ratio.

Dividends & Buybacks

Sempra pays a roughly 3.4% dividend yield now, after 15 consecutive years of dividend increases, aiming to deliver ~50-60% of its earnings, which is not a terrible yield, but quite low in a high-rate market where the nearly risk-free US treasuries pay better. They stopped the buybacks back in 2023 when the investment cycle began. Still, there is potential to see their EPS in the long term, so this dividend could be much more meaningful by the end of the decade.

Risks & Opportunities

Sempra’s Q4 report made the stock crash ~19% when the earnings came short of the market’s expectations, cutting the 2025 guidance after citing “regulatory matters and the backdrop of a higher-cost environment,” as well as higher interest expenses after higher capital investments in 2025. Still, their Q1 report reaffirmed their updated outlook for 2025 and 2026, and helped them guide towards the higher-end or even above the projected EPS CAGR of 7-9% for 2025 through 2029, which is actually a very significant level of growth.

As part of their plan - which I also talk about in the paragraph below - they target $13 billion of investments this year, which is significantly above last year’s ~$10 billion capital investments. As per their report, they also plan to sell non-core assets in Mexico and continue their Fit for 2025 initiative to further reduce costs and improve productivity. So, it seems like they might be at the high-end of their latest investment cycle that started in 2023, when CAPEX jumped by about $3 billion (~55%) compared to 2022 and 2021. This significant potential to grow, combined with a relatively attractive valuation and exposure to key industries, are a reason why I think Sempra might be a Buy now.

Their major opportunity comes from the Texas transmission expansion plan. As the largest player in Texas’ energy transmission and distribution through Oncor, they expect to build a significant portion of the project, which is, in part, something that explains the higher investments planned. They presented a $56 billion investment plan (vs $48 billion announced in Q3; primarily focused on regulated utilities, with 90% of investments dedicated to California and Texas) back in February, of which ~$36 billion will go to Oncor, meaning a 50% increase compared to a year ago. The company already achieved record growth last year, although the 2025 guidance was lowered - as I mentioned above - to reflect regulatory impacts in California, higher costs in Texas, and delays in LNG projects. Still, their long-term goals are what I’m interested in, and I wouldn’t mind short-term pain that could unlock better prices, but I’ll detail that in the valuation segment. Of course, you can imagine how rate cuts could significantly help them with such a significant investment plan in progress, and I believe we’ll most likely see that in the near future, especially as Powell’s term expires in May 2026 and Trump (who’s already calling for rate cuts) already has some replacements ready.

Long-term, energy is literally a vital side of any society, especially if we consider the tailwinds that support it. For example, data centers consumed about 4.4% of the total US electricity consumption after tripling over the past decade, and now the US Department of Energy says it is projected to double or triple by 2028 - expecting it to consume from 6.7% to 12% of total US electricity by 2028! Of course, the other sources of demand are likely to not disappear - quite the opposite - meaning there’s a significant need for more and more energy production, which can be very beneficial for a company like Sempra. To capitalize on this, they could also potentially look into a nation-wide expansion in the long term, but I believe that would be more likely done through a merger rather than direct investments, especially since Sempra has a massive investment plan in development now and plans to divest non-core assets to focus on it. Still, the potential remains in the long-term, so that’s maybe another catalyst for long-term growth, especially if they manage to deliver synergies.

Another long-term opportunity for Sempra could come from their LNG exposure. Currently, the LNG market is in a very interesting spot following the war in Ukraine, which led to a very significant drop in EU’s imports of Russian gas and a 119% jump in LNG imports from the US in 2022 compared to 2021. This fell by ~19% in 2023 and continued to grow in 2024, with the exports of US LNG to Europe (6.3 Bcf/d) still significantly higher than in 2021 (2.4 Bcf/d). This resulted in significant jumps in profit and cash flows for LNG-related companies, with Cheniere Energy’s (LNG) free cash flow jumping 5.8 times in 2022 compared to 2021, for example. That then led to higher investments to capitalize on this sudden shift in the market, but these projects take time.

In the future, if this dependency deepens, the US could continue using LNG as a bargaining chip in future US-EU trade deals, gaining significant importance and potentially bringing more support from the state. Trump has already been in favor of pushing these LNG projects thanks to their strategic importance, with the potential to see the country’s production capacity double by 2030. Still, there is a risk of seeing further declines in these projects (which we’ve seen especially during Biden’s term), which can continue to put pressure on the market’s supply and demand balance, although I believe the long term is in favor of the commodity and I remain bullish on it. Sempra is also one of the companies that jumped on the trend, with their Port Arthur LNG project in Texas together with ConocoPhillips (COP) being already fully subscribed to ConocoPhillips, RWE (OTCPK:RWEOY), Orlen (OTCPK:PSKOF), INEOS and Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY). Japan’s JERA also joined the list recently with a 20-year LNG supply deal!

However, there’s also risks, of course, on top of the competition. We recently saw California cutting the amount requested by Sempra’s SoCalGas in their General Rate Case, approving a revenue requirement of $3.806 billion for SoCalGas, meaning ~$629 million (~14.2%) less than Sempra sought. This was 9.3% lower compared to 2023, which could be an issue if it continues in the long-term, potentially making the company miss its targets (already helped cause it to lower the 2025 guidance). For a bit of clarity, every 4 years, SoCalGas and SDG&E file a General Rate Case with the CPUC, where they propose how much money they need to collect from customers (called their revenue requirement) to cover the company’s operating costs/maintenance/improvements and, of course, a profit (the rate base). So, any cut here means a cut to Sempra’s profits, and it also opens the potential for long lawsuits with the state, as we’ve seen before. And the same for Texas. Any cut in their costs or intention to save money - which could even delay projects or lower their EPS - has the potential to lead to a miss/cuts in their guidance, which we know already that the market really doesn’t take well.

Valuation

Valuing Sempra based on a DCF model doesn’t make much sense since the company has almost never reported a positive FCF, as it requires reinvestments to secure regulated earnings. Instead, since there’s plenty of relatively predictable regulated equity earnings, I believe their earnings could be a good indicator in this case.

Using their 2024 earnings of $2.817 billion, I went for a CAGR of 8% annually for 5 years - the midpoint of their target - followed by 5% annually for the next 5 to take into account the industry’s potential to grow beyond 2030, plus a 3% terminal growth rate once they reach a more mature state, and a 6.25% WACC - in line with market expectations (see here and here). Accounting for $36.3 billion in net debt and ~652.17 million shares outstanding, the model returns Sempra’s equity value at ~$84.56 billion, meaning an intrinsic value per share of about $129.66.

That’s about 72% above the current value, which may seem like much, but keep in mind that if the market cap doesn’t improve, and they reach their growth target, we’re looking at earnings of ~$4.346 billion based on the assumptions above. The current market cap would mean they’re traded at a ratio of ~11.4, which is very cheap compared to the current 17.65 and a 10Y median of ~19.

Conclusions

Overall, I think Sempra is definitely an interesting company to consider. There’s good potential to grow thanks to the massive investment plan, they offer exposure to key markets and states, on top of a decent dividend and an overall solid balance sheet traded at quite an attractive valuation. As such, I rate Sempra a Buy now, although we have to remember that there is still risk, and everything depends on their plan to grow in a volatile market.

data.title: Rates Spark: Can Payrolls Hang In There?
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, and Benjamin Schroeder

A super-weak payrolls report can create more problems for Treasuries than face value suggests

There's quite a soft market expectation from Thursday's payrolls number. At around 100k, that's some 50k short of the normal replacement rate in the 150k area. As a consequence of this shortfall, the unemployment rate is anticipated to rise to 4.3%. And this combination comes against a backdrop of rising jobless claims, and the beginnings of a notable strike higher in continuing claims. Neither of these are at real danger levels, but they are indicative of a slow creak in the labour market. The evidence does not point to a negative print (fall in employment), but it is noteworthy that Wednesday's ADP report did, in fact, print negative.

What is quite remarkable is the juxtaposition between this slowing macro environment versus a degree of relative optimism in the risk assets space. Equity markets appear to be cheered by the prospect of Fed cuts to come, and high-yield spreads are back down at levels that point to a reduced default risk (in turn impliedly suggesting not a significant recession risk). From a US 10yr Treasury perspective, any sense of super-weakness in the payrolls report would present a recipe for a downward lurch in yields, on a theory that the economy is shuddering and the Fed may need to accelerate its rate cutting agenda. By the same token, a number of between 100-150k would be deemed tolerable enough, and one not requiring deep worry on the economy.

That all being said, we also can't ignore the Big Beautiful bill and the fiscal legacy of it, which is an elevated fiscal deficit. For now, this is being broadly ignored. But bear in mind that macro weakness would also mean a shortfall in tax receipts, adding to upward pressure on the fiscal deficit. That's an important sting in the tail for Treasuries to consider, from a supply perspective and an implied credit rating one. On top of that, we'll see upward pressure on headline inflation rates in the coming months too as the tariff effects begin to bite, which is another issue for Treasuries to be concerned with. Remember, 10yr SOFR today is 3.7%. The question is whether that is sustainable should US inflation head towards 4% through the third quarter (which we think it will). That's the complication. The gut feeling is we should be bullish on Treasuries on macro risks. But the head is screaming that this may not be the way to go this time around.

Fiscal concerns, (another) UK edition

Fiscal concerns around the UK sparked noticeable bear-steepening of the gilt curve, with 30y yields rising by as much as 20bp, some 12bp more than Bunds and Treasuries that day. It was a reaction to the latest headlines around the position of Chancellor Reeves as Prime Minister Starmer failed to provide his backing - a watered-down welfare bill had earlier this week brought into question her fiscal consolidation plans.

This also marks a stark reversal in the dynamics relative to Bunds. Gilts had previously managed to tighten versus Bunds over the past month and particularly the past week. That can be in part rationalised by Germany’s outsized borrowing plans recently outlined by the government pressuring Bunds, and gilts receiving tailwinds from Bank of England commentary suggesting that the central bank’s active gilt sales could be scaled back in the future. However, the main driver of the spread tightening, for lack of other news - until now - was a relatively higher correlation of gilts to US Treasuries, and the latter experiencing a rally on the back of softer data and dovish Fed voices.

This latest episode highlights markets’ ongoing sensitivity to the trajectory of government finances. It is something that we think also puts US Treasuries still at risk of a sell-off episode, though we are now experiencing a more macro-driven market dynamic. The German government’s plan for new borrowing of €850bn until 2029 is already pressuring Bunds, though crucially, the country is looking at a very different starting point. With the new 10y Bund benchmark that was launched on Wednesday, the 10y Bund yield is now 4bp above the swap rate. We think in the longer run, Bunds can still cheapen as the supply pressure builds. A further 10bp increase of the spread looks possible, taking into account not only the rising net issuance but also the declining bond portfolio of the ECB.

Thursday’s events and market view

The US will release its official job numbers a day earlier than usual. This is a key input to the recent discussion around the possibility of accelerated and deeper Fed cuts. The consensus expectation is still for a 110k increase in non-farm payrolls, with estimates ranging from 70k to 160k. Even after the surprisingly weak ADP figures, Bloomberg’s whisper number (basically every Bloomberg terminal owner can provide his estimate) slipped to only just below 100k. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate is seen ticking slightly higher, from 4.2% to 4.3%. Other US data to watch is the ISM services, which economists see climbing above 50 again, if only barely so.

The eurozone will see the final services and composite PMIs for June, while the European Central Bank publishes the accounts of the June policy setting meeting.

In primary markets, Spain and France will auction bonds: Spain from 3y out to 15y maturities (up to €6.75bn including linkers), and France from 10y to 30y maturities (up to €12bn).

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Groupon: Beware The Surge In Optimism
data.description:
Sometimes, amid a sharply rising stock market, the best performers may surprise you. Though it has been large-cap growth stocks that have dominated the S&P 500's performance to fresh new records, Groupon (NASDAQ:GRPN) has actually made a far more astounding comeback.

The local deals site has seen its share price nearly triple this year as the company reported stabilization in billings, and in particular managed to reverse recent trends and start adding to active paying customers in North America again. The question for investors now is: does Groupon have further room to rally, or is this a dead cat bounce for a company that has long passed its peak popularity?

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Groupon in March, when the stock was trading at $15 per share. Needless to say, by cautiousness on the stock, while seemingly rational at the time, caused me to miss out on fantastic gains. And yet, I do think that the market has overreacted sharply in sending Groupon stock substantially higher in the wake of minor boosts to recent billings trends. When we add in macro uncertainties on top of Groupon's own historical choppiness, in taking a fresh look at Groupon at current share prices, I remain neutral on the company's prospects.

First and foremost, the core reason to invest in Groupon would be its cheapness as a value stock, so let's start with a discussion of Groupon's latest multiples against its latest outlook. At current share prices near $34, Groupon trades at a market cap of $1.37 billion. After we net off the $301.6 million of cash and $246.5 million of convertible debt on Groupon's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $1.31 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, Groupon has flexed up its full-year outlook to 3-5% y/y growth in billings, versus a previous 2-4% range. It has maintained the rest of its outlook flat at $70-$75 million in adjusted EBITDA, the midpoint of which represents a 14.6% margin, and $493-$500 million in revenue (0-2% y/y growth). As a reminder, Groupon reports "billings" when customers actually buy deals and coupons on its site, while revenue is recorded upon use.

Groupon trades at 18.1x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. The stock is no longer a value play after its ~3x surge this year, despite its stabilization in North American customers and billings. Yes: the company still has an opportunity to greatly expand its adjusted EBITDA if it can truly sustain billings growth and hit meaningful economies of scale. But I think it's a mistake to invest in Groupon while it's priced like a growth stock, merely because the company had a single good quarter of customer results and a very slight boost to its billings expectations. It's difficult for me to shed my impression of Groupon as a legacy platform that is unlikely to stage a major comeback to popularity.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere while monitoring Groupon from the sidelines.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Groupon's latest Q1 results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Groupon's revenue declined -4% y/y to $106.3 million, slightly beating Wall Street's expectations to $104.3 million (-6% y/y) by a two-point margin. Results improved slightly versus -5% y/y decline in Q4.

Where investors mostly cheered, however, was Groupon's surprising comeback to billings growth - which is a strong forward-looking indicator of revenue growth (as deals purchased this quarter will eventually get recognized as revenue as they are consumed). In the North America segment, billings of $256 million grew 11% y/y and accelerated three points versus 8% y/y growth in Q4. In the international segment, excluding the Italy division which Groupon exited, billings of $80 million grew 5% y/y, also accelerating 3 points versus 2% y/y in Q4.

Another positive indicator was the company's bounce back in active customers, particularly in North America. The company added 200k net-new active customers in North America in Q1, ending the quarter with 10.5 million (+3% y/y); while the international segment still continued to lose 0.1 million net customers.

Q1 is notable for its return to overall customer growth after several quarters of decline, but we note that Groupon has a rather loose definition of "active customer" that simply means an account that made a purchase sometime in the last twelve months.

The company believes that its "hyper local" strategy of driving appealing offers within specific target markets is driving strong user activation. Per CEO Dusan Senkpyl's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Our hyper local strategy is delivering strong results with our top 10 cities in North America, growing billings by double digits. Enterprise clients continue to flourish on our platform. Looking at our vertical performance within North America Local, Things to Do, grew billings double digits for the 5th straight quarter.

We believe our Things to Do franchise is significantly outpacing current industry trends and is poised to continue strong absolute and relative performance as we head into the important summer season.

Still, it is also possible that the company had a lot of one-time strength that won't carry forward: especially if macro headwinds intensify and reduce billings strength in the back half of the year.

On the profitability front, we note that despite billings growth and a more modest revenue decline, Groupon's adjusted EBITDA declined -22% y/y to $15.3 million, or a 14.4% margin that declined -220bps y/y.

Groupon's midpoint guidance at 14.6% margins is slightly above its actual results in Q1, so the company will need to continue driving economies of scale through the balance of this year in order to hit its own expectations.

Key takeaways

In my view, Groupon's rally has taken the stock way ahead of its skis. Investors are reacting way too optimistically to a minor increase in full-year billings expectations and an upswing in North American active customer counts that macro headwinds could knock off course again. With valuation multiples at ~18x forward adjusted EBITDA despite a history of choppy execution, I'd recommend avoiding this stock for now.

data.title: Tradeweb Government Bond Update - June 2025
data.description:
As central banks held their monetary policy meetings across the globe, the evolution of government debt market yields was varied across jurisdictions in June. The European Central Bank kicked things off on June 11 with its fourth rate cut this year, citing easing inflation.

In contrast, the U.S. Federal Reserve held rates steady on June 18 for the fourth consecutive meeting. Similarly, the Bank of Japan and Bank of England kept rates unchanged on June 17 and June 19, respectively.

Beginning in the U.S., the yield on the 10-year Treasury benchmark note ended the month at 4.23%, down more than 19 basis points from May 30.

Bhas Nalabothula, Head of U.S. Institutional Rates at Tradeweb, said: “June saw the continuation of geopolitical unrest in the Middle East, heightened by the Iran/Israel conflict. On June 24, the Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell confirmed at its Congressional testimony that a higher outlook for inflation was causing the central bank to keep rates unchanged for the foreseeable. At the same time, the U.S. 10-year Treasury reached a month-high of 4.51% on June 6, while the 10-year and 30-year yield spread was at its most inverted level in June since early October 2021, registering at 56 basis points at month-end.”

Yields in the Eurozone reflected a mix of upward and downward shifts. Belgium’s 10-year government bond yield rose 12 basis points to 3.15%, and its Austrian equivalent followed with an eight-basis point rise to 2.97%.

Germany saw a more modest increase of under two basis points, bringing its 10-year Bund yield to 2.53% at month-end. The country’s inflation rate eased to 2% in June, according to preliminary data. In the same month, the HCOB Germany Manufacturing PMI reached a 39-month high of 49.

Over in the UK, the 10-year Gilt yield increased by 17 basis points to 4.49%. Household disposable income in the country fell at its fastest rate of 1% in two years in the first quarter of 2025, while in May, the inflation rate edged down to 3.4% from 3.5% the month prior. The S&P Global UK Manufacturing PMI rose to 47.7 in June from 46.4 in May, and the GfK Consumer Confidence Index increased by two points to -18 in the same month.

The French 10-year government bond yield climbed 10 basis points to 3.27% at the end of June. The annual inflation rate in France rose to 0.9% in the same month, ending the over four-year low of 0.7% seen in May. In contrast to its French counterpart, the Italian 10-year benchmark bond yield dropped by nearly three basis points to 3.46%. Italy’s inflation rate inched higher to 1.7% in June from 1.6% in the previous month.

Moving to the Asia Pacific, Japan's 10-year government bond yield finished June eight basis points lower at 1.43%, easing after May’s nearly 20 basis point surge. While long-end yields had previously reached record highs in May, June saw more subdued movement as the Bank of Japan maintained interest rates and the country’s inflation rate eased to 3.1% year-over-year during the month.

In Australia, the 10-year benchmark bond yield dropped by almost nine basis points to 4.25% at month-end, marking a reversal from the 19 basis point rise seen in May. The inflation rate in Q1 this year registered at 2.4%, which is unchanged from the previous quarter.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Private Credit Outlook: 5 Lessons Learned
data.description:
By Matthew D. Bass

Some say private credit hasn’t been tested. We disagree… and stress can sharpen the senses.

The last few years have delivered plenty of stress for investors. First, a global pandemic—then a sharp rise in inflation and interest rates. Sweeping US tariffs on a lengthy list of goods surprised markets in the spring, with wide-ranging effects on the global economy and investment landscape.

Because prices in public markets are more observable, these shocks have translated into sizable bouts of volatility across equities and fixed income—including credit. As has been widely discussed in the press, private market volatility has seemed relatively muted—but that doesn’t tell the whole story. Now, with equity indices rebounding to new highs, inflation declining, and stability seemingly taking hold, some might conclude it's back to business as usual.

Quite the contrary. If anything, the past five years have underscored that private credit is not immune to market cycles. But periods of stress often yield lessons—and opportunities—for nimble investors willing to adapt.

As we reflect on recent events and beyond, here are five key takeaways:

1) It’s All About the Alpha

Corporate direct lending isn’t exotic anymore. Today, it's a core allocation within many diversified fixed-income portfolios. While performance across the asset class has been strong over many years—largely supported by accommodative monetary policy—it’s misguided to believe that a rising tide will continue lifting all boats.

One defining feature of the US corporate direct lending market is its fragmentation: different sectors, sponsors, sizes, structures and solutions, with direct lenders competing for deal flow within and across these segments. Based on a recent S&P Global analysis of middle-market collateralized loan obligation data, the percentage of portfolio holdings that managers have in common remains extremely low. Simply put, not all direct lenders are alike. Channels for originating deals, underwriting approaches, sector preferences and, as a result, the companies in portfolios vary quite a bit.

As default rates and other signs of stress emerge across this asset class, the impact is anything but uniform across companies and managers. In short, consistent historical returns may have fostered a perception of private credit as a beta-driven market—but looking ahead, we believe alpha will be the key differentiator.

2) Recurring Revenue: It’s Not Created Equal

We’ve long believed in the value of lending on a recurring revenue basis to Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) providers that offer critical solutions for enterprise resource planning. However, not all recurring revenue is created equal, and revenue quality demands careful attention.

As interest rates and the cost of capital soared, the surge in tech spending that began during the pandemic—when employees shifted from office to home—began to wane. Elevated rates also compressed SaaS company enterprise values, tempered growth trajectories and pressured customers, many of which began scaling back spending on software licenses.

Many lenders that extended too much leverage during the low-rate environment have learned this lesson the hard way. From our perspective, this experience is a clear example of a high-growth sector where underwriting experience and generating alpha through asset selection are more important than ever. Lenders must first identify businesses with resilient value propositions and a demonstrated ability to navigate cycles—then calibrate loan sizes accordingly.

3) Disruption Once Again Breeds Opportunity

Here’s a revealing stat: private equity funds distributed just 14% of their net asset value (NAV) to investors last year, down from an average of 28% per year from 2015 through 2020 (Display). Macro headwinds—including rising rates and political uncertainty—have dampened M&A activity and price discovery. As private equity continues to mature as an asset class, and with both general partners (GPs) and limited partners (LPs) seeking liquidity, private fund finance has grown across a variety of structures. These range from traditional capital call facilities to more innovative solutions such as GP- and LP-led secondaries and continuation vehicles.

With banks still under pressure, alternative lenders have been driving this growth, and fund finance has become a rising allocation in investors’ portfolios.

Limited Private Equity Distributions: Private Credit Opportunity

One area where we see tremendous opportunity is within private equity NAV lending. Much like the rise of the capital call facility market, we believe NAV lending will become a core financing solution for private equity sponsors to optimize liability management throughout a fund’s lifecycle. From an investment perspective, these structures often offer attractive risk-adjusted returns supported by low loan-to-value ratios and healthy yields, creating compelling return profiles that can complement corporate direct lending exposures.

4) Diversification, Diversification, Diversification

While disciplined underwriting and robust risk management are table stakes for any top-tier asset manager, it’s essential to guard against the unexpected. As we see it, this is where diversification reigns supreme.

As the private credit market has expanded across sectors and regions, investors now have more tools than ever to diversify their exposure. Consider the asset-based finance market, which has grown to more than $6 trillion—many times larger than corporate direct lending—and provides much of the financing for a diverse range of non-corporate spending, including consumers and small businesses. Commercial real estate debt—with certain sectors more acutely impacted by the pandemic and interest rate volatility—has continued to show signs of stabilization, and investors’ interest is reawakening in this additional avenue for diversifying private credit exposure with distinctive risk-return dynamics.

Structural innovation is also playing a role. Perpetual or evergreen vehicles enable the construction of highly diversified portfolios over longer time frames than traditional drawdown structures. In our view, today’s private credit investors are better equipped than ever to diversify—whether it’s from a top-down view across geographies and sectors or a bottom-up view within a single fund.

5) The Consumer Isn’t a Monolith

The sprawling consumer sector remains the heart of asset-based finance, and higher-for-longer interest rates have caused stress. But the details matter when it comes to consumer underwriting—whether it’s in the US or Europe. That puts a premium on underwriting experience and insight.

Loan purpose and repayment hierarchy matter. For example, auto loans typically rank high in repayment priority, whereas loans aimed at consolidating credit card debt warrant greater scrutiny. In Europe, lending dynamics differ from country to country—what works in Germany may not work in Ireland. What’s more, European loans are often more complex operationally, but that complexity may also command higher yields.

A Final Thought: Market volatility isn’t likely to fade anytime soon. But if recent history has taught us anything, it’s that private credit continues to offer compelling risk-adjusted return potential—and a strong case for its thoughtful inclusion in portfolios.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams, and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Is Thermo Fisher A Buy? A Deep Dive Into Growth, Valuation, And Moat
data.description:
In the past, I have covered two companies that belong to the diagnostics and research market. Sometimes the market is also referred to as the Life Sciences Tools and Services market, and while I have only written once about Danaher Corp (DHR), which is right now the second-biggest company in this market according to market capitalization, I frequently covered Waters Corp (WAT) (right now, the ninth-biggest company according to market cap).

But I never covered the current market leader – Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (NYSE:TMO) with a current market capitalization of $158 billion. In the following article, we will answer the question if Thermo Fisher also has a wide economic moat around its business and whether the stock is a good investment right now. But we start with a short business description.

Business Description

Thermo Fisher provides life sciences solutions, analytical instruments, specialty diagnostics and laboratory products. The company was formed in 2006 through the merger of Thermo Electron (founded in 1956) and Fisher Scientific (founded in 1902). Today the company, which is headquartered in Waltham, Massachusetts, has about 125,000 employees and was generating $43 billion in revenue in fiscal 2024.

The company serves several end markets, but the biggest part of revenue is generated from pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies (57% of total revenue). And academic and government institutions, diagnostics and healthcare institutions and industrial companies each contributed about 15% of revenue. And while 52% of revenue is generated in North America, about 25% of revenue stems from Europe and 19% from the Asia-Pacific region.

Thermo Fisher is reporting in four different business segments. The biggest segment (at least when looking at revenue) is Laboratory Products & Biopharma Services, which generated $23.1 billion in revenue in fiscal 2024. However, this segment has a low operating margin of only 13.3% in fiscal 2024 and therefore an operating income of $3,090 million. Life Sciences Solutions is the second-biggest segment by revenue and generated $9.7 billion in fiscal 2024. This segment, however, is extremely profitable and due to a 36.4% operating margin, the segment generated $3,503 million in operating income. The other two segments are Analytical Instruments, which generated $7.5 billion in annual revenue in fiscal 2024 and Specialty Diagnostics, which generated $4.6 billion in fiscal 2024.

Growth Slowing Down

When looking at the last ten years, Thermo Fisher increased its revenue with a CAGR of 10%, adjusted earnings per share with a CAGR of 12% and free cash flow also increased with a CAGR of 12% from $2.4 billion in 2014 to $7.3 billion in 2024.

However, when looking at the last quarterly earnings, the picture is a little different. Instead of double-digit growth, revenue increased from $10,345 million in Q1/24 to $10,364 million in Q1/25 – resulting in 0.2% year-over-year growth. While revenue increased only slightly, operating income grew 3.2% year-over-year from $1,663 million in the same quarter last year to $1,716 million in this quarter. Diluted earnings per share grew 15.0% year-over-year from $3.46 in Q1/24 to $3.98 in Q1/25.

While earnings per share grew in the double-digits, free cash flow – one of the most important metrics – declined steeply. Instead of $908 million in free cash flow in Q1/24, it was only $373 million in Q1/25 – a decline of 58.9% year-over-year.

Balance Sheet

In the last decade, Thermo Fisher was also growing by acquisitions, and the company made several “expensive” acquisitions. For example, in 2023, it acquired Olink Holding for $3.1 billion and The Binding Site Group for $3.5 billion. In 2021, Thermo Fisher acquired PPD, Inc. for $17.4 billion.

In February 2025, Thermo Fisher announced it entered into a definitive agreement with Solventum (SOLV) to acquire Solventum’s Purification & Filtration business for about $4.1 billion in cash. The business segment has about 2,500 employees and is generating an annual revenue of $1 billion.

And while growth by acquisition can be a solid strategy, it has a negative impact on the balance sheet. On March 29, 2025, Thermo Fisher had $2,819 million in short-term debt as well as $31,370 million in long-term debt. When comparing the total debt to a total equity of $49,357 million, we get a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.69, which seems acceptable.

However, when comparing the total debt to the operating income (which was $7,849 million) and free cash flow (which was $6,724 million), we should at first subtract $4,134 million in cash and cash equivalents as well as $1,813 million in short-term debt. When comparing $28,242 in net debt to the operating income, it would take 3.6 years to repay the outstanding debt. When comparing it to the free cash flow, it would take 4.2 years to repay the outstanding debt.

Aside from the debt levels, there is another problem sitting on the balance sheet. Thermo Fisher also has $46,493 million in goodwill on its balance sheet – 47% of total assets are therefore goodwill, and this is certainly not what we like to see. By the way, when subtracting goodwill from the company’s assets, total equity results in less than $3 billion.

As long as Thermo Fisher can continue growing at a high pace, these metrics are not problematic. However, the current debt levels are not perfect and considering that Thermo Fisher is struggling a bit right now, we should keep a close eye on the debt levels.

Dividend and Share Buybacks

Acquisitions are one way to use the generated cash and allocate capital. Another way is dividends and share buybacks. Right now, Thermo Fisher is paying a quarterly dividend of $0.43 resulting in an annual dividend of $1.72 resulting in a dividend yield of 0.4% and therefore the dividend is not really a reason for investors to buy the stock. However, Thermo Fisher can be seen as a dividend growth company with a dividend growth of at most 15% annually in the last five years.

Aside from spending about $500 million annually on dividend payments right now, Thermo Fisher is also buying back shares. In fiscal 2022 and fiscal 2023 the company spent $3 billion on share buybacks and $4 billion in fiscal 2024.

Since the merger in 2008, the number of shares outstanding declined, but at a rather slow pace. The number of shares outstanding declined about 0.9% annually, but the number of shares outstanding reached a low in 2013 and increased again due to the acquisition of Life Technologies Corporation.

Growth Potential

In the last ten years, revenue grew with a CAGR of 9.76%, but I have my doubts if Thermo Fisher is able to repeat these growth rates. As pointed out above, acquisitions played an important role for growth and with debt levels and goodwill being rather high, the company should not buy other companies at a similar pace. Or if it is making acquisitions, these acquisitions should not be financed by taking on high levels of additional debt, but increasing the number of shares (another way to finance an acquisition) also has a negative impact on the bottom line.

When talking about the growth potential of Thermo Fisher, the current budget cuts by the Trump administration seem to be a threat for growth. As mentioned above, 15% of the company’s revenue is generated by government or academic institutions and in the last few months billions in funds were frozen and spending was cut aggressively. But recently, it was announced that the NIH has issued a memo advising against cancelling any more research projects. This decision also seems to be in-line with a judge in Boston deeming the grant cuts illegal.

And while this seems to be good news for companies like Thermo Fisher, we should also always keep in mind that predictability is not a goal the Trump administration is aiming for and in a few weeks from now the situation could be completely different again.

However, analysts are still optimistic for the years to come and between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2025, earnings per share are expected to grow with a CAGR of 8.57%. When looking at the earnings revision data in the last few quarters, we see analysts getting more pessimistic.

While analysts are expecting growth rates in the high single digits, management seems to be much more optimistic and is expecting organic revenue growth between 7% and 9% for the long run. And in combination with margin improvements as well as substantial capital deployment (which could mean share buybacks as well as acquisitions) earnings per share as well as free cash flow could be expected to grow in the mid-teens.

Wide Economic Moat

I already wrote articles about similar business in the past – one article about Danaher Corporation and several articles about Waters Corporation. And in both cases I argued for a wide economic moat around the businesses, and I would make a similar argument for Thermo Fisher.

We could argue that the brand name might also play a role and generate a structural advantage for Thermo Fisher, as some customers (mostly companies as well as government and academic institutions) might buy the products due to the brand name. But the major source for a wide economic moat around the business are switching costs. And here we are dealing with two different sources of potential switching costs.

First, if an academic institution or pharmaceutical companies has bought instruments and equipment from Thermo Fisher, its staff is trained on these instruments, it is likely that the equipment will be replaced with products from Thermo Fisher again. Switching costs also arise from the fact that Thermo Fisher is offering mission-critical equipment. In my article about Danaher Corp, I explained:

These instruments are absolutely necessary for a laboratory to function well, and consumers are probably hesitant to buy low-quality (and therefore cheaper) products. The switching costs arise when a lab or healthcare provider makes the purchase, as these equipment and tools often have steep learning curves. It often takes a lot of time and money to train the staff on the equipment, and companies are very reluctant to double these costs and train the staff again on the equipment of a competitor.

But not only the equipment will probably be replaced by products of Thermo Fisher again. As I have already mentioned above, only 17% of revenue stems from instrument sales. But after the instrument sale, the customers are essentially locked in and now must purchase additional consumables for the instruments and in most cases, they also have been bought from Thermo Fisher. This is leading to 83% of revenue being recurring revenue for the company and stemming from service and consumables. This is leading to a huge strategic advantage for the business.

When looking at the qualitative description of the business, Thermo Fisher most likely has a wide economic moat around the business. However, when looking at quantitative numbers, the picture is not so clear anymore. In the chart above, we see the gross and operating margin being more or less stable, which is a good sign for a wide economic moat – of course, margins were not the same every single year, but we don’t see huge fluctuations.

When looking at return on invested capital, the average RoIC in the last ten years was 8.64% and 10.25% on average when looking at the last five years. These are still solid returns the company generated, but we usually assume that a company with a wide economic moat around the business should be able to generate exceptionally good returns and a RoIC in the high single digits is rather average than exceptional.

We can also look at the stock performance compared to the S&P 500 and companies with a wide economic moat should outperform the market. In the case of Thermo Fisher, we clearly see an outperformance since the early 1990s. While the S&P 500 increased 1,630% in value, Thermo Fisher increased 4,580% in value and this is already a clear outperformance. And we must keep in mind that Thermo Fisher declined almost 40% from its all-time high, while the S&P 500 is at all-time highs.

All in all, I see Thermo Fisher having a wide economic moat around its business and being able to fend off competitors quite well, despite the return on invested capital not being exceptionally high.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

However, a final question remains: Is the stock a good investment, and even a high-quality business can be a horrible investment at some points in time. Therefore, we must calculate an intrinsic value and look at valuation multiples. At the time of writing, Thermo Fisher is trading for 24 times earnings and while this does not seem cheap on an absolute basis, it is close to the lowest P/E ratio in the last ten years and clearly below the 10-year average of 32.51. When looking at price-free-cash-flow, the picture is similar. Right now, Thermo Fisher is trading for 23 times free cash flow, which is close to the lowest P/FCF ratio in the last ten years (around 19) and below the 10-year average of 28.33.

Aside from using simple valuation multiples, we are also calculating an intrinsic value by using a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we calculate with a 10% discount rate, as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least. We are also calculating with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding, which was 379 million at the end of Q1/25.

As basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $6,724 million. Now we must determine a growth rate for the next few years that seems reasonable. In line with analysts’ assumptions, let’s assume 8% growth for the next ten years followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $385.71 for the stock, and Thermo Fisher is still slightly overvalued.

Of course, we could make the argument for higher growth rates, and the company’s own long-term outlook is aiming for EPS and FCF to grow in the double-digits. Let’s be more optimistic in one calculation and assume 12% growth for the next ten years, followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This would lead to an intrinsic value of $503.92 and the stock and Thermo Fisher would therefore be undervalued.

Additionally, we can also make the argument that Thermo Fisher is a high-quality business with a wide economic moat around the business and in the case of high-quality businesses, we can also calculate with a growth rate of 6% till perpetuity.

Conclusion

Thermo Fisher is certainly a great business with a wide economic moat around its business. However, I see the stock as fairly valued at best, as I don’t see the company growing in the double digits in the years to come. In the last ten years, the acquisition contributed to growth but due to rather high debt levels and very high amounts of goodwill on the balance sheet, I don’t know if Thermo Fisher can continue this strategy. Therefore, I rather see growth rates in the high single digits being reasonable and the stock will remain on my watchlist – together with Waters Corp and Danaher Corporation.

data.title: ServiceNow: Growth Runway Is Still Long And Bright
data.description:
Investment Summary

My recommendation for ServiceNow (NYSE:NOW) is a buy rating. I believe the growth runway still remains very healthy, as I see NOW's execution in AI monetization and vertical expansion as clear, tangible drivers that can sustain 20% revenue CAGR. This dynamic should support the premium valuation it currently trades at.

Business Overview

NOW's core product is its Now Platform, which enables enterprises to digitize and automate workflows. The business was known for its reputation in IT Service Management [ITSM], but over the years, it has evolved into what it calls "the AI platform for digital transformation." The idea is that by seamlessly connecting disparate systems and automating processes from front office to back office, NOW positions itself as the "enterprise operating system."

Explosive AI Momentum Signals a New Growth Chapter

In my view, NOW's growth outlook is extremely long and bright, driven by its AI strategy. For reference, per the investor day, management guided for AI-related revenue to reach $1 billion in Annual Contract Value [ACV] by the end of FY26, which marks an impressive 4x increase from the $250 million in AI ACV reported in 1Q25. And I am optimistic that NOW can achieve this.

The reality is that customers are recognizing the value of NOW's agentic AI capabilities (included in the premium Pro Plus SKUs), and more importantly, the financial impact is significant. Firstly, per management, NOW is seeing a 25% average selling price [ASP] uplift when customers upgrade from the Standard to Pro tier. Secondly, and arguably more significant, is the transition to Pro Plus, which drives an additional 30% uplift. Which means the total price uplift that NOW sees is ~60% when customers move directly from Standard to Pro Plus. We know customers are double upgrading, as >15% of upgrades in FY24 were these "double upgrades".

This dynamic should drive both revenue growth and margin expansion in the coming years, providing a clear runway for earnings growth acceleration. This impact should become increasingly sizeable as adoption of AI features ramps up when AI becomes central to customers' digital transformation strategies.

Hybrid Pricing Model De-Risks Adoption and Unlocks New Revenue Streams

NOW's hybrid pricing model makes this AI growth story even more attractive. By combining the typical seat-based subscriptions with a flexible, consumption-based pricing component for AI usage, NOW has effectively removed a key barrier for enterprise AI adoption. How it works is that when a customer subscribes to Pro Plus licenses (this gives NOW a steady stream of revenue for a start), they are also allocated "assist packs," which they can test out AI features. This basically gives customers a "free" trial of how good NOW's AI features are. As they see tangible return-on-investments [ROI], they are likely to scale their consumption, and the beauty is they can do this by simply purchasing more "assist packs," which removes the friction of complex renegotiations or the constraints of the typical annual renewal cycle.

This model is already working out nicely, as evidenced by the reported 50% month-over-month growth in Now Assist consumption. This indicates that customers are not only adopting these features but also expanding usage rapidly. Strategically, this makes a lot of sense as well, because it enables NOW to reap the monetization benefits quicker-it can capture incremental revenue without waiting for renewal events.

Breakout Growth Beyond ITSM

As I mentioned, NOW has moved beyond ITSM, and this pivot has been successful. A key proof point is that >50% of new logo ACV in FY24 came from outside the core IT domain. To be fair, more has to be done and shown to instill more confidence that NOW can become a strong player outside ITSM, but the potential is immense. NOW focuses on 4 primary verticals: technology, CRM and industry, core business, and creator workflows. And it has done very well so far. Take the CRM and Industry Workflows vertical, for instance; it has surpassed $1.4 billion in ACV and has >30% y/y growth. The Core Business Workflows vertical performance is also noteworthy, as it has crossed $1.1 billion in ACV, and it is still accelerating, with net new ACV growth exceeding 40% y/y in 1Q25.

This diversification is critical because of multiple reasons. Firstly, it expands NOW's addressable market; secondly, it significantly enhances NOW's monetization potential as it is able to tap into larger budget pools and drive larger enterprise agreements. Lastly, as these non-tech workflows contribute more meaningfully to the revenue mix, NOW growth can stay elevated at ~20% for a long period of time.

Valuation

I believe NOW can continue to print 20% revenue CAGR for the foreseeable future, driven by strong visible growth drivers that are already contributing to growth. Compared to other infrastructure software peers like Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), and Palo Alto Networks (PANW), NOW has a much stronger growth outlook (20% vs. mid-to-high teens), and this is a key reason why I think NOW can continue to trade at 14x forward revenue (peers are at low teens). Moreover, NOW profitability (GAAP EBIT margin) should also start to inflect higher as revenue scales (was 12.4% in FY24 and 14.6% in 1Q25), which justifies a higher valuation vs. history.

Risk

While NOW has a strong market position, this is still an inherently competitive industry with several large enterprise software vendors. Should the competitive pressure step up, NOW may face limits in pricing growth. Aside from that, macroeconomic factors such as FX volatility, inflation, and a tight labor market could dampen new business growth, impacting the pace of large deal signings. Additionally, NOW's premium valuation creates exposure to multiple compression if growth decelerates unexpectedly.

Conclusion

My view for NOW is a buy rating because its AI momentum, hybrid pricing model, and diversification beyond ITSM are driving durable, high-margin growth. With a clear runway for 20% revenue CAGR and margin expansion, I believe it justifies its premium multiple.

data.title: Central Bank Gold Buying Picks Up In May
data.description:
Global central banks bought a net 20t in May based on reported data, close to but still below the 12-month average of 27t (Chart 1).1 Fresh tensions in the Middle East may have reinforced the strategic appeal of gold for central banks looking to safeguard reserves against geopolitical shocks. This continued interest in gold was also highlighted in our recently released Central Bank Gold Reserves Survey 2025. Gold remains a focus for central banks worldwide, with 95% of respondents believing that official gold reserves will continue to increase, up from 81% last year. A record 43% of central bankers also indicated that their own gold reserves would rise over the next 12 months.

Chart 1: Central bank net purchases ticked up in May

Kazakhstan leads central bank gold purchases in May

In May, these central banks have reported changes (of 1t or more) to their gold reserves:

  • National Bank of Kazakhstan (NBK) reported adding 7t of gold to their reserves in May, lifting them to 299t. Since the start of the year, NBK gold reserves have increased by 15t
  • Central Bank of Turkey reported 6t of gold purchases this month, lifting their y-t-d gold accumulation to 15t
  • National Bank of Poland also added 6t of gold to their reserves in May; the NBP remains the largest net purchaser of gold in 2025, adding 67t
  • People's Bank of China and Czech National Bank both added 2t of gold respectively this month, followed by three other central banks which added 1t of gold each this month: National Bank of the Kyrgyz Republic, National Bank of Cambodia, the Central Bank of the Philippines and the Bank of Ghana
  • The Monetary Authority of Singapore led sales this month with 5t, followed by Central Bank of the Republic of Uzbekistan and the Deutsche Bundesbank, each sold 1t of gold this month.2 On a y-t-d basis, Uzbekistan remains the largest net seller (27t) followed by Singapore (10t) (Chart 2)
  • Updated data for April 2025 also showed that the Qatar Central Bank purchased 2t of gold, bringing global net purchases that month to 16t.

Chart 2: Central banks continue their broad accumulation of gold in 2025

Y-t-d central bank net purchases and sales, tonnes*

Our recently released Central Bank Gold Reserves Survey 2025 reinforces gold's relevance to the central bankers worldwide, with key insights highlighted below:

  1. Record participation and growing engagement

    a. A record 73 central banks participated in our survey, reflecting continued interest in gold as a strategic reserve asset

  2. Accelerated accumulation trends

    a. Over the past three years, central banks have purchased more than 1000t of gold annually, compared to just 400t-500t per year in the prior decade

  3. Optimism towards gold holdings

    a. 95% of respondents expect global central-bank gold reserves to rise in the next 12 months

    b. 43% anticipate an increase in their own holdings - up from 29% in 2024 - a record high; EMDE banks show stronger intent than advanced economies

  4. Structural shift in reserve portfolios

    a. 76% project gold will constitute a moderately or significantly higher share of total reserves over five years (up from 69%)

    b. 73% expect a reduced share of US dollar reserves in the same period

  5. Enhanced active management

    a. 44% now actively manage gold separately (up from 37% in 2024)

Footnotes

1Average monthly net purchases between May 2024 and April 2025.

2The Bundesbank sale is likely related to its longstanding coin-minting programme.

Original Post

data.title: Margin Pressures And The Path Forward For Herc Holdings
data.description:
Executive Summary

Herc Holdings (NYSE:HRI) is a large equipment rental company with a highly diversified fleet and customer base. It serves U.S. and international customers across the construction, industrial and infrastructure markets. The company’s growth outlook rests on (1) continued strength in large “mega-project” rentals (data centers, infrastructure, onshoring) which are supported by federal funding; (2) a pipeline of acquisitions (notably the pending H&E deal) and expansion of higher-margin specialty equipment; and (3) improving pricing and utilization as local weather/headwinds ease. Critically, Herc’s balance sheet is highly leveraged (debt/Equity ~372%) which means higher interest costs and sensitivity to demand swings. In Q1 2025 revenues grew modestly (~5–7% YoY) as harsh winter weather and local-market softness weighed on branch utilization. Management expects mid-single digit growth for 2025 overall, driven by recovery in the national accounts and infrastructure sectors. At current valuations (P/E ~29×, EV/EBITDA ~7×) the stock trades rich to industrial rental peers, implying high expectations for growth. Key risks are slower local construction (interest-rate impacts), integration/execution of deals, and any pullback in government-funded projects.

Company Overview and Background

Herc Rentals Inc. is one of North America’s largest equipment rental companies, founded in 1965 and based in Bonita Springs, Florida. The company rents a broad range of construction and industrial equipment, from aerial lifts, earthmovers and compressors to specialized climate-control, power-generation and trench shoring units, along with related services (maintenance, training, transport). It also sells used equipment and supplies. Herc’s business is split roughly 60/40 between local contractors (residential/non-residential construction, maintenance) and large national accounts (massive infrastructure, manufacturing and energy projects). This diversification has given Herc resilience: for example, demand from government-funded infrastructure and energy projects can offset slower private nonresidential starts. The company has grown both organically and via M&A (50+ acquisitions over the past decade), adding branches and fleet in attractive regions. Herc’s most recent corporate strategy emphasizes cross-selling specialty equipment to its expanded customer base and leveraging technology (its ProControl account management, digital rental platform) to improve utilization. As of early 2025, Herc employs ~7,200 people and operates several hundred branches across North America (with a small international presence). Its value proposition is broad rental capabilities with a one-stop offering of equipment and services; this contrasts with traditional construction equipment manufacturers or commodity suppliers.

Strategic Position and Future Outlook

Herc aims to capitalize on a multiyear pipeline of “megaprojects” and infrastructure spending. Recent forecasts (e.g. Dodge Data) show nonresidential construction and infrastructure starts picking up in 2025, driven by federal stimulus and corporate on-shoring. Industry surveys (e.g. the Baird/RER survey) expect U.S. rental revenues to grow roughly +5–7% in 2025. In particular, large industrial, energy and infrastructure projects (data centers, chip plants, pipelines, broadband build-out) are proceeding apace, and Herc is targeting a 10–15% share of these opportunities. On new mega-projects, Herc is seen favorably because of its national account expertise and broad fleet (including recently scaled specialty units). For example, Baird notes that infrastructure and industrial markets have the most positive outlook for 2025 due to government funding. In parallel, Herc is integrating the pending H&E Equipment acquisition (6% national market share combined) to expand its branch network and national accounts. Management expects >60% of the H&E revenue synergies to come from cross-selling its specialty equipment to H&E’s customers over 2–3 years.

Meanwhile, Herc’s local market (smaller commercial construction, maintenance) remains weak due to high-interest rates. Demand for smaller projects has slowed, and pricing is pressured by idle surplus fleet in the industry. Herc’s own CEO commented that there has been “no real significant change” in local-market softness, but that the company’s broad sector mix (muni work, education/healthcare projects, utilities) has helped mitigate it. Weather disruptions in Q1 2025 (cold snaps in January/February) temporarily hurt utilization, but utilization began normalizing in March. Herc’s management is addressing the mixed environment by (1) leaning into winning large-project contracts, (2) rotating older equipment out of the fleet and expanding into higher-margin categories, and (3) maintaining price discipline (monitoring rate competition and equipment supply). Herc also notes that tariffs on imported equipment have minimal near-term impact (most of its fleet is sourced domestically and 2025 prices were locked in), although any indirect effect via customer projects is uncertain.

Financial Health

Herc’s balance sheet is heavily leveraged by recent acquisitions. As of Q1 2025, total debt was roughly $5.05 B against only $48 M cash on hand (implying debt/equity ~372%). This includes a large asset-based credit facility and term loans (Herc’s proposed new term loan was rated ‘BBB-’ by S&P). The company’s interest expense burden is substantial (trailing 12-month interest ~$261 M), which pressures net income (Herc actually reported a first-quarter loss after heavy acquisition-related costs). On the plus side, interest rates on much of the debt are fixed or LIBOR-linked, and Herc maintains liquidity via its large credit lines. Nonetheless, its net debt/EBITDA is well above industry averages (roughly 3.2× according to analytics), leaving little margin for error.

Equity and book value are small relative to fleet assets. Herc’s trailing book value per share ($47.65) is far exceeded by the stock price ($131). The company’s cash flow generation is decent: 2024 free cash flow was positive (operating cash ~$1.16 B vs. capital spending $1.22 B). Herc recently declared a quarterly dividend of $0.70 (annualized $2.80, ~2.1% yield) and has modest share buyback authorization, but returning cash is limited by debt covenants and the desire to fund fleet growth.

Profitability margins are average for a rental company. In 2024, Herc’s rental gross margin was around 35–40% (similar to other rental firms, given equipment payments), and operating margin about 14–15%. These margins are lower than asset-light peers but typical for equipment rental. Herc’s EBITDA has grown with new projects; 2024 EBITDA was ~$857 M (about flat YoY excluding one-time items). Return on equity (~9.6% TTM) is modest, reflecting the large equity base and debt load. In short, Herc’s finances are stable but stretched by debt. Its strong points are solid operating cash flow and ongoing project funding; its weak points are low liquidity (current ratio ~1.5×) and high leverage, meaning any downturn could stress the balance sheet.

Financial Performance and Comparable Analysis

Herc’s recent financial performance reflects resilient revenue but lumpy profitability. On a trailing-12-month basis through Mar 2025, total revenue was about $3.62 B (up 7% YoY). Rental income grew with new branch acquisitions and project volume, but Q1 2025 net income swung negative due to one-time acquisition costs and heavy non-cash depreciation. Operating cash flow remains healthy ($1.16 B TTM), supporting about $226 M levered free cash flow. Herc’s core EBITDA margin (~24% of revenue) is in line with or slightly above industry peers that run large fleets.

By valuation, Herc trades at a premium to most rental and industrial distributors. Its current P/E (~29× TTM) and EV/EBITDA (~7×) are higher than smaller peers (e.g. Boise Cascade at ~5.5× EBITDA) and even higher than large peer United Rentals (URI trades around ~12× EV/EBITDA). This reflects market expectations of higher growth from Herc’s backlog and M&A. Below is a summary table of key ratios for Herc (HRI) and selected comparables (GMS, Rush Enterprises, Boise Cascade, McGrath RentCorp, MSC Industrial). These peers operate in adjacent industrial/rental markets.

Metric (TTM) HRI GMS RUSHA BCC MGRC MSC
Latest Revenue (USD B) 3.62 5.51 7.78 6.62 0.92 3.75
Revenue Growth (%) +7.1% -5.6% -1.1% -6.6% +4.0% -4.7%
Valuation
Price/Earnings (TTM) 29.3× 33.3× 14.5× 11.0× 12.2× 22.5×
EV/EBITDA 7.16× 12.27× 8.04× 5.56× 7.36× 13.38×
Price/Book 3.09× 2.61× 1.86× 1.57× 2.56× 3.51×
Price/Sales 1.09× 0.70× 0.55× 0.52× 3.16× 1.28×
Profitability
Profit Margin (%) 3.53% 2.09% 3.76% 4.72% 25.81% 5.69%
Operating Margin (%) 14.75% 4.68% 4.96% 3.55% 23.32% 7.14%
Leverage & Liquidity
Debt/Equity (%) 371.7% 112.5% 79.1% 24.7% 49.2% 43.2%
Current Ratio 1.50× 1.91× 1.44× 2.82× 2.03× 1.92×
Returns
Return on Equity (%) 9.60% 8.02% 14.20% 14.26% 22.80% 15.18%
Dividend Yield (%) 2.08% 0.00% 1.39% 0.95% 1.64% 3.99%

Herc’s profitability (operating margin ~15%) is much higher than GMS, Rush or Boise, reflecting Herc’s more comprehensive service offering. Its net margin is modest. Herc’s valuation multiples are also relatively high, P/Sales ~1.1× vs 0.5–0.7× for the large equipment distributors, indicating investors expect above-trend growth. Herc's debt/equity (~3.7×) far exceeds peers. Even highly leveraged comparables like GMS (112%) or Rush (79%) are much lower in relative terms. This superior liquidity risk is partially offset by Herc’s large asset base and access to credit, but it does suggest higher vulnerability in a downturn.

Key Risks and Challenges

Herc’s performance hinges on several risk factors. The largest risk is a sustained pullback in construction/investment spending. If the U.S. were to enter a recession or if infrastructure projects are delayed, Herc would see lower demand both from national accounts and its legacy local customer base. Prolonged high-interest rates could further delay local projects and reduce maintenance capex (reducing rentals). Herc’s heavy debt means it is exposed to rising rates and refinancing risk. A sharp rise in rates or credit-market squeeze could increase interest costs or limit access to capital. Any cost overruns on new projects might strain its credit profile. The H&E acquisition is large and complex. Failure to effectively integrate locations and fleets could erode expected synergies. Even smaller bolt-on acquisitions in the past have had transition costs.

Conclusion

Herc’s recent results show steady rental revenue growth and record backlog, and its management has demonstrated discipline in capital allocation (pausing non-core M&A, focusing on integration). The company’s capital structure is stretched. The 2024–25 acquisition spree has left Herc with very high leverage, so future growth must also focus on deleveraging. Herc's free cash flow can fund some debt paydown, but new fleet investment (for specialty and replacements) will keep capex high.

Herc trades at a premium relative to larger rental peers, implying that the market expects significant growth ahead. That growth can come from completion of large construction projects and integration of recent acquisitions. If Herc can execute on these fronts and if infrastructure/industrial spending remains robust, the stock has upside. Conversely, if rental demand disappoints or debt costs surge, the premium valuation would be at risk.

Herc offers investors exposure to a diversified equipment-rental business poised to benefit from major construction and infrastructure initiatives. Its advantages are scale, specialized offerings, and a strong backlog; its drawbacks are cyclical end markets and a lofty debt load. As one analyst put it, the rental market is currently oversupplied, but sentiment is improving. For long-term investors, Herc’s success will depend on both industry recovery and management’s ability to convert future cash flows into deleveraging and growth.

data.title: AMDY: Price May Have Finally Stabilized
data.description:
Overview

YieldMax AMD Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:AMDY) offers investors a way to collect a large distribution rate from indirect exposure to Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). Now that AMD's price seems to have stabilized, I believe that AMDY offers investors an attractive value proposition at the moment. I previously covered AMDY and issued a buy rating due to the market's mispricing on AMD. Since then, the total return sits above 14% when including distributions, which outpaces the growth of the S&P 500 over the same period. Now that some time has passed, I wanted to revisit this option ETF to provide some updated insights into its outlook, income utility, and risk profile going forward.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see how AMDY's price has experienced rapid deterioration and has now fallen by more than 52%. Even when including all distributions that were paid out over the same time frame, AMDY's total return is still at a loss of 13.4%. However, I chalk these declines up to the external influences within the technology sector, rather than some underlying flaw of AMDY itself. The technology sector moves rapidly and is highly reactive to different influences, but AMD has been more reactive than usual due to its association within the AI sector.

Based on AMDY's latest declared distribution, the estimated annualized distribution rate now sits around 77%. With a distribution this large, there are quite a few creative ways to utilize the income received. I believe that a fund like AMDY cannot be viewed through a traditional lens, and it requires a long-term outlook to be a profitable position. However, I want to emphasize that AMDY presents a higher level of risk than some other YieldMax funds that track stocks with better price stability. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that AMDY implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy & Risks

AMDY operates a synthetic option strategy that aims to deliver large distributions from the premiums it collects. The fund essentially harnesses the underlying volatility of AMD to execute its options. The term 'synthetic' means that AMDY doesn't actually hold any common shares of AMD to execute its strategy, so the fund is writing options against an asset that it doesn't own. The synthetic nature of the fund has the potential to significantly increase the risk profile for investors, as AMDY's price can be more volatile than AMD's share price. Since AMDY doesn't actually own the shares, it needs to get creative in order to replicate the daily price movement of AMD.

To mimic daily price changes, the fund simultaneously purchases ATM (at-the-money) call options and sells put options. In order to generate the option premiums that are used to fuel distributions, AMDY sells OTM (out-of-the-money) call options that typically have a strike price somewhere between 0% and 15% above the current market price of AMD. This allows the fund to collect income while also participating in a bit of capital appreciation during strong bull runs. The prospectus highlights the underlying strategy in greater depth and confirms that AMDY has a total operating expenses of 1.23%.

AMD currently trades around $139 per share and to help demonstrate how the strategy works, let's imagine that AMDY will write an option against AMD and select a strike price of $145 per share. If AMD has a strong quarterly earnings and the share price increases to $150 per share, AMD's option would be executed at the selected strike price. This means that AMDY's option strategy sacrifices all of the upside price appreciation above the strike price, which limits how well AMDY can participate in the price growth of AMD. This is exactly why I would anticipate AMDY to always underperform AMD from a price perspective.

For instance, AMDY price has declined by more than 60% since its inception back in September 2023. However, AMD's price has increased by about 36.7% over the lifespan of AMDY, which effectively demonstrates the capped upside that AMDY will experience. Although the upside is capped, the downside is not. For instance, we can see that AMD's price remained on a consistent downtrend from March 2024. AMDY followed the same price movement downward over the last year, but wasn't able to efficiently capitalize on the price recovery as markets started to rise.

This price decay is a common theme for many of the YieldMax funds. These option ETFs have proven that it very unlikely for them to recover from large price declines, unless the underlying common equity that it tracks experiences a rapid price movement higher. There are several different examples we can find by looking at the price performance of other YieldMax ETFs. Option ETFs that have experienced a similar decline are as follows:

  • YieldMax COIN Option Income Strategy ETF (CONY)
  • YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY)
  • YieldMax AAPL Option Income Strategy ETF (APLY)
  • YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY)

As we can see above, all of these peer funds have experienced large double-digit declines. Even an option ETF based on Apple (AAPL) has experienced a similar decline, which reinforces that continuous price declines are a natural side effect of the large distributions that YieldMax funds pay out. Therefore, the main risk is that investors can see their invested capital decline over time if AMD's share price isn't in an uptrend. It is a very unfortunate downside to these funds, so I hope that management figures out a way to better preserve investor capital at some point in the future.

Another risk would be underperformance compared to AMD. The company continues to deliver strong revenue growth and profitability metrics. I anticipate that AMD will continue to deliver positive results as the AI sector maintains its rapid growth rate over the next decade. With that being said, a position in AMDY is not the best way to gain exposure to the growth of AI, as the ETF is likely to underperform despite its massive distribution yield of 77%. As we can see below, AMDY still underperforms common shares of AMD, due to the large price downside that was experienced.

Performance

Despite offering investors a large distribution rate, AMDY's performance has been lackluster. For instance, the price has declined by 61% since the ETF's inception. Even when including all distributions paid out, the total return sits around 10.4%. However, I believe that the price decline over the last year can be attributed to external influences, rather than some underlying weakness of AMD.

From a valuation perspective, AMD has been mispriced for a long time now. The business has great growth metrics, healthy operating margins and has market share in a sector that is projected to show meaningful growth over the next decade. Despite this, AMD's price maintained a downward price momentum from April 2024, up until April 2025, and I believe it may be related to the following reasons:

  • Perceived Competition In The AI Space: Investors were busy focusing on whom the winner of the AI wars was going to be. Capital piled into fast movers like Palantir (PLTR), and ignored companies that had strong fundamentals, such as AMD.
  • Influence From Analysts: Firms were downgrading AMD's rating and slashing price targets.
  • Fear Of China: Chinese competitor DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) shook markets with their LLM (large-language-model) that proved to be as efficient as ChatGPT at a fraction of the cost. This caused investors to question whether or not all the capital flowing into AI was really worth it.

These points may not be entirely false, but AMD remained extremely profitable through all of it. Now that there is a lot more optimism around the sector and AMD's share price is now seeing some positive momentum, AMDY's price may finally change directions and provide better stability going forward. Therefore, I will now be initiating a position in AMDY in anticipation of the price remaining more consistent.

Distribution Utility

As of the most recently declared distribution of $0.4629 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 77%. It's important to note that the distribution rate will change every four weeks after a new payout is announced. Therefore, the listed distribution rate may be different by the time you read this article. The unfortunate byproduct of AMDY's price suffering from a large decline is that the distributions also become smaller. As we can see below, payouts were once as high as $1.67 per share. Therefore, there's always a risk that AMDY's payouts can get smaller over time if the price decline were to continue.

As the NAV declines, the fund will have to adjust its payouts lower. However, we can also see that the payouts have started to trend back upward now that AMDY's price has maintained some positive momentum. While I have zero expectation that payouts get back to their prior levels, it's always a possibility if AMD were to experience a large run-up. However, I believe that YieldMax can be extremely forgiving; the longer you hold them because the more payouts you receive, the closer you get to getting your invested capital paid back to you in distributions.

AMDY launched around $20 per share. Since its inception, AMDY has paid out a grand total of $15.1535 per share in distributions. The breakdown by year is as follows:

  • 2023: $1.5609 per share
  • 2024: $11.1185 per share
  • 2025 (YTD): $2.4741 per share

Once the fund has paid back a total of $20 per share in distributions, investors would have essentially reached 'house money' status. Once achieving a holding period where your initial capital is paid back to you, your risk from that point forward is significantly reduced. This is because you would have received your initially invested capital back, but you still own the shares that are producing your income every four weeks.

One of the most attractive aspects of receiving income from AMDY is that the distributions are generally considered to be tax-efficient. For instance, the latest distribution email indicates that AMDY's last dividend was made up of 96.14% of return of capital. Return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. Therefore, AMDY can be efficiently utilized within a regular taxable brokerage account with very little tax burdens.

The screenshot above is from an email that YieldMax sent me. I would suggest that you reach out to the YieldMax team on their site and request to be added to their distribution list. This is the best way to get the most accurate distribution breakdowns that include how much ROC a payout consists of.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on AMDY as I believe the price may have finally stabilized. AMD's fundamentals remain solid, and the optimism around its ability to remain competitive in the AI space has improved. Even through the large price decline, AMDY has been able to provide a positive total return through its large distributions. However, there are still plenty of risks for investors to consider. For instance, the payouts can continue to decline if AMD's price momentum shifts back to a negative sentiment. Additionally, AMDY is very likely to underperform AMD over a long period of time, assuming that AMD will deliver positive price growth in the future. The capped upside nature of the option strategy limits the capital appreciation that can be experienced, and YieldMax ETFs have a reputation for their inability to regain price momentum over the long term.
